title: OpenAI previews GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna for ~20 trusted partners
subtitle: The three-tier successor to GPT-5.5 introduces a new 'max' reasoning effort and an 'ultra' subagent mode — but at the U.S. government's request, access starts behind a vetted allow-list rather than a public rollout.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: OpenAI on June 26 began a limited preview of GPT-5.6 in three named tiers — Sol (flagship), Terra (balanced), and Luna (fast and cheap) — available only through the API and Codex to about 20 pre-approved organizations. Per-million-token pricing posted to OpenAI's documentation puts Sol at $5 input / $30 output, Terra at $2.50 / $15, and Luna at $1 / $6, with Terra landing at roughly half the cost of GPT-5.5 for comparable everyday work. The release also adds a new 'max' reasoning effort that gives Sol more deliberation budget on hard problems, and an 'ultra' mode that spins up subagents to split a single task across parallel workers.

What makes this a release worth tracking is the gating, not the model card. OpenAI confirmed that the trusted-partner restriction was put in place at the U.S. government's request and that the company shared the models and rollout plan with federal officials before the preview opened. That mirrors the posture Commerce took with Anthropic's Mythos 5 — frontier models cleared by allow-list, not general API key. Two of the three labs at the global capability frontier are now operating under a vetted-customer regime in the same week.

The mechanical changes — ultra mode, max reasoning, cheaper Terra — line up with the bigger industry shift toward intelligence-per-dollar. Subagents inside a single model call recognize what enterprise teams have been building on top of Claude and Gemini for the last six months. Cutting Terra's price in half is a direct answer to open-weight competitors like Zhipu's GLM 5.2 and DeepSeek, both of which have eaten into OpenAI's mid-tier business by running on customer hardware at a fraction of frontier API cost.

A takeaway for learners: the choice of model size now matters more than choice of provider. If you can do your work on Terra or Luna, you are paying a fifth to a third of Sol's rate for performance that is probably good enough — and OpenAI is telling you with this pricing that they expect most enterprise work to land there. Practice picking the smallest model that actually clears your task's quality bar; that skill compounds as the price gap between tiers widens.
